moment_text: Most impactful point actually was definitely my brother's incident to get me into caregiving, but I would actually say what stands out the most to me is actually when my stepdad passed from prostate cancer. Having the knowledge that I know and going through it again unpacks differently and. I really constantly always see how women carry the full burden. You know, there's this saying, what is it? You burn the candle on both ends. But I feel, uh, specifically as a daughter in that point, I saw how my mom was carrying the candles, right? And how I needed to be there to support her as much as possible in that journey. So for me. In regards to mental health and seeing it from a caregiving lens couldn't be even more important than anything in your everyday life.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains emotional signals (overwhelm) and a system interaction (family), highlighting the pattern of caregiver burden.

moment_text: I'm constantly always reminded myself, right, how do I tend to this garden and how do when new things enter my life? How do I recalculate today? It could be my stepdad. But there's always something coming into our life that is going to shift us and our mental health is what grounds us with that. I heard this saying and I just, it stays in my mind continuously. It's like when you're driving and you make a wrong turn, it doesn't say bad. It says Recalculating. It's okay. We just have to make another turn. And so as anything that happens in our life, especially things that can weigh on us that are heavy, just like caregiving, how do we recalculate and keep moving through it? Because it is our grounding and how we navigate and look at things from a perspective of. What is positive in it? I did a talk just the other day about caregiving, tending to your garden. One of the key factors in that is weathering the storm, and at first when I was riding it, I said, weathering the storm. We all know that it's gonna happen and it's gonna be heavy. But then I really looked at it from another perspective of sometimes in that storm there is peace. If I really literally think about when there is a storm, there is a peace in that there is a rebuilding that has to happen. And I think all, all of that said in one is my inflection. And it's kind of ironic right now. Today is the aftermath of Hurricane Melissa in Jamaica. And, um, you know, embracing all the emotions that is a fear for my family there and friends and what that is gonna be like. And so it's, it's just always a journey in life. And our mental health must always be at the forefront.
eligibility_rationale: Fails A2 evidence gate: While emotional signals are present, the system_interaction is 'none', making it a personal philosophy rather than an observation of a systemic or relational pattern.

moment_text: It was totally different experiences with my brother. I didn't, mental health was not something that was talked about when I was in my twenties. That's just how I am. If you tell me a sad story, I'm gonna be boo-hooing and my mom knows that of me. And so I remember the day we got to the I, I. So, let me back up. My brother's accident happened in Indiana. My mom lives in New Jersey. I was in Jamaica at the time and we all had to fly in. So when I finally arrived, like a day later after he's been in the hospital, I wanted to go see him immediately and my mom stopped me. And she said, you're not allowed in this room, Michelle, until you can control your emotions. Because anything that comes in this room has to be of positivity and I know you're gonna cry. And at that moment, to me, I was sad, hurt. You know, I wanna see him, I wanna be present. I'm like looking through like the window. I just want to be there. But now knowing all the things and have done the work in regards to mental health, doing the work is so important of knowing your emotions and how to feel, and I had to feel my way through that and sit in it, you know, to really say, okay, there's other things I can do to support my strong mom here who doesn't cry. From that, from outside and I was able to kind of help navigate the friends that were coming to visit. 'cause you know, he's 21, there was 101 fraternity brothers and all the things, you know, trying to navigate that piece for her that that was my role. I'm happy I got to do that at that time. But I wasn't prepared. I didn't have the toolkit at that time

moment_text: with my stepdad. That was just four years ago. It felt different. It was a very hard time. moment_text: You know, with Fastest Labs, I am using it in a way to help my patients. So one of the ways, you know, I, I'm a certified dementia practitioner, and with dementia, I'm very passionate about it. My mom's oldest sister is in a memory care community, and seeing her dementia and seeing it, the signs early. And then seeing her progress through the disease is, is very hard to witness as well as just, that's who I see on a normal basis for, for other families. And with dementia, I think it is innate. When I meet the caregivers of the anxiety, they feel like, how do I prevent this? From happening to me. Like what do I need to do, Michelle, to prepare myself, right? And I have these conversations often, and so Fastest Labs has given me the toolkit to offer PGX testing and it's pharmacogenetics testing. And I think when you're working with dementia patients, they don't know or they can't communicate how medication makes them feel. And when you have someone with dementia, they are on medication and many times what I have found is their medication might not be best for them. There may be alternate that works better for their DNA. And the PGX test allows us to just do a cheek swab and test to see, uh, how that medication reacts to their DNA. And we have seen significant improvement. By just giving that now to their primary care physician to say, can we alter this medication? This one gives them side effects, and this one doesn't, and I think everyone needs it, but more so for those persons who can't speak to how they feel.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains clear barrier signals (lack_of_language), agency signals (self_advocacy), and emotional signals within a healthcare_provider system interaction.

moment_text: For example, Elizabeth, you know, you may take a medicine and say, Hey, doc. It's kind of giving me a stomach ache. It's making me too tired. You know, they adjust the milligrams and that's just, that's how we work with medicine. We just adjust based on how we communicate it, affect how it affected. And sometimes just seeing the signs aren't enough. Uh, and I do believe that many times we, you know, especially with dementia patients, they can be overmedicated. Uh, and so that gives me the toolkit to add to what we are doing with right at home in addition to just compliance. You know, I feel that the biggest tool or my product is caregivers. And I wanna make sure, hands down, that everyone is receiving the same care I would give to my mom and my dad.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: Contains barrier signals (lack_of_language, misdiagnosis) and agency signals within a healthcare_provider system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The barrier 'misdiagnosis' is used here to capture the concept of overmedication as a form of incorrect treatment.

moment_text: It's funny you say that 'cause it speaks to this quote that I always, uh, see and it's caregiving is love in action, but for women it's often invisible labor. And so we want to be seen. Everyone wants to be seen. You wanna have that feeling, but caregiving is an invisible labor that you're not getting any rewards for. And when I think of the journey for women. And there's always the term sandwich generation, caring for your parents and then caring for your kids. But that's not it. That's not encompass of everything. Because I witness women caring for their kids, caring for their parents, and then also caring for their spouse, their partner. You're talking about a triple effect. That is, that is labor intensive, and that is so many hours of care, understanding, tasking, outsourcing, managing them and yourself. It it, it's unspeakable how I witness that.
eligibility_rationale: Passes evidence gate A2: Contains barrier signals (lack_of_validation, lack_of_support) and emotional signals within a family system interaction.
